one_line_takeaway: DiffReach unifies Taylor-model flowpipes and CROWN NN bound propagation into one differentiable, GPU-batched JAX reachability primitive, making certified reachable sets fast enough (~100x over DT CROWN) to regularize NN model training and drive uncertainty-aware MPC on robots up to 72D and on Kuka iiwa hardware.
key_finding: The unified linear-TM + CROWN engine matches or beats existing GPU reachability tools while being differentiable: vs discrete-time CROWN reachability it achieves comparable tube tightness at roughly 100x lower runtime, and vs the continuous-time interval tool immrax it produces substantially tighter reachable sets at comparable runtime (immrax returns infinite-volume tubes up to 95% of the time from numerical overflow). Differentiability enables refinement (input splitting and gradient descent) that shrinks reachable-set volume by ~5x (91.94 -> 18.18) and, embedded in training, produces models with far tighter certified tubes at only slight nominal-accuracy cost, which in turn lets reachability-aware MPC keep higher task success under large disturbances where vanilla MPC on non-certified models degrades badly (especially the quadrotor).
core_contribution: The paper introduces DiffReach, a GPU-parallel, differentiable reachability engine (built in JAX) that computes sound reachable-set over-approximations for closed-loop robotic systems whose dynamics and/or controllers contain neural networks. Its technical core is a fixed-shape Taylor-model (TM) representation, F(z)=c+Az (+ B tau z) with an interval remainder, that unifies Taylor-model flowpipe construction for continuous-time analytical dynamics with CROWN-style backward linear bound propagation for neural components, sharing a single linear-TM interface so that neither side is intervalized between modules. On top of this primitive the authors build DiffReach-Robotics: (1) a certified training method that adds a differentiable reachable-tube-volume regularizer (Eq. 9) plus a horizon/perturbation curriculum to standard prediction/tracking losses, and (2) a reachability-aware sampling-based MPC that penalizes candidate action sequences by their certified reachable-set violation and refines the top candidate with gradient descent. The unifying claim is 'verified-by-construction' learning and online planning, where reachability directly informs training and MPC rather than being a post-hoc check.
threat_model: Not a security paper. There is no adversary. 'Robustness' here means soundness of set-based over-approximations under bounded, non-adversarial uncertainty: the initial state lies in a box B_epsilon(x0) modeling sensing/modeling error and disturbances (e.g., 0.05 perturbation to quadrotor position/velocity, +/-0.1 rad/s to joint velocities, 0.05/0.015 for T-pushing planning/control layers). No attacker, no adversarial-example threat model is invented. NOTE: the source record's abstract was corrupted with fragments lifted from cited references (e.g., 'adversarial examples via the convex outer adversarial polytope' is reference [16], Wong & Kolter, not this paper's contribution); the paper itself makes no adversarial-security claim, and its cluster_label 'Adversarial Robustness' overstates the security relevance.
limitations: Guarantees are only with respect to the learned dynamics model, not the real system (the authors note calibrated model-error bounds via conformal prediction could be folded in, but this is not done). Long-horizon continuous-time conservatism remains a problem: at 5x horizon on the quadrotor the CT-controller reachable-volume ratio blows up to 18.6154 (splitting only partly mitigates to 6.2407), and 42% of CT trajectories become unstable/infinite-volume without splitting (24% with splitting). The method targets smooth or learned dynamics and explicitly does not handle hybrid or contact-rich dynamics. Fixed-shape TMs restrict the polynomial part to linear/quasi-quadratic forms, trading tightness for GPU efficiency (memory still O(n^2)). Hardware evaluation is qualitative only, and tightness on articulated systems (10-joint arm) depends heavily on splitting.

datasets_used: No standard ML datasets; self-collected simulation trajectories from random-interaction rollouts
T-pushing manipulation task (2D pushing, Pymunk-style physics)
Single quadrotor (12D state) with analytical ODE dynamics
Coupled 6-quadrotor swarm (72D state, 18D action) with spring-like coupling
Kuka iiwa hardware platform for real-world T-pushing MPC rollout
Certified-training eval used 1,000 random initial sets X0 and test trajectories
quantitative_results: DT reachability: comparable tube tightness to CROWN baseline at ~100x faster runtime
CT reachability: substantially tighter than immrax at comparable runtime; immrax returns infinite-volume tubes up to 95% of trials
Refinement (Table I): baseline volume 91.94 at 0.0021s; input splitting(8) volume 27.56 at 0.0048s; gradient refinement(20 iters) volume 18.18 at 0.1084s
Runtime (Table II): 1 quad 12D sampling 0.2601s vs ours 0.1399s; 6 quad 72D sampling 2.2455s vs ours 0.1976s
6-quadrotor swarm: average 1.37x expansion on position dimensions vs 100k-sample dense (under-approx) baseline
10-joint arm (Table III): EEF X/Y width sampling(100k) 0.4799/0.6256 at 1.0944s; ours no-split 0.8414/1.2264 at 0.0531s; 64 splits 0.8118/0.9756 at 0.1186s; 512 splits 0.7353/0.8999 at 0.3854s
Long-horizon quadrotor tightness (Table IV, volume ratio vs sampling): DT Dyn no-split 1.3221 (1x) -> 1.8898 (5x); CT Ctl no-split 1.8648 (1x) -> 18.6154 (5x); CT Ctl 8-splits(rpy) -> 6.2407 (5x)
Unstable CT trajectory fraction reduced from 42% to 24% via roll-pitch-yaw splitting
Hardware MPC on Kuka iiwa runs online at ~10 Hz (5 Hz with gradient-based refinement)
Reachability over horizon 10 with 128 randomly sampled X0; DT model = 3 hidden layers of 96 units; all on RTX 4090
baselines_compared: Dense Monte Carlo sampling (100k samples, under-approximation) - conservativeness reference
Sampling-based rollout (MPPI [61] / CEM [43] style) - MPC runtime
Vanilla MPC with non-certified/regular models (no reachability constraints) - task success
Vanilla planning with certified models (ablation, no reachability penalty) - task success
Prior TM+CROWN NNCS templates POLAR [24] / CROWN-Reach [73] cited as interval-interface predecessors (conceptual comparison)
